title: Graph on the Coordinate Plane
why: Before you graph points anywhere on the coordinate plane, you need the basics solid: read a number line, and plot a point by going across (x) first, then up (y) in the first quadrant. Warm those up and graphing clicks.

diagnostic.q: On a number line, what whole number sits exactly between 2 and 4?
To plot a point, which do you do first?
A point is 3 right and 2 up from (0,0). What are its coordinates?

learn.heading: Across then up
learn.intro: A coordinate point is written (x, y). The x tells you how far to move across (right). The y tells you how far to move up. Always do x first, then y.
examples: (4, 1): start at (0,0), move 4 right, then 1 up.
Reading a point: if a point is 2 across and 5 up, its coordinates are (2, 5).

tiers.intro: Let's read the number line one step at a time.

items.q: A. On a number line, what number is 3 steps right of 0?
B. To plot (5, 0), how far do you move across (x)?
items.hint: The first number is x = 5, so move 5 right.

items.q: C. To plot (5, 0), how far do you move up (y)?
items.hint: The second number is y = 0, so you don't move up at all.

items.q: A. A point is 2 right and 4 up from (0,0). What are its coordinates?
B. In the point (6, 3), what is the y-coordinate (how far up)?
A. Which point is farther to the right?
B. A point is at (4, 4). If you keep y the same but move 2 more right, what is the new x?

exit.q: 1. A point is 5 right and 2 up from the origin. What are its coordinates?
exit.hint: x first (5 across), then y (2 up) → (5, 2).

exit.q: 2. In the point (8, 3), what is the x-coordinate?
